title: COPD — Exacerbation
oneLiner: Acute worsening of chronic airflow limitation, usually triggered by infection, causing dyspnea, cough, and sputum change; a leading cause of emergency admissions.

sections.1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You:
**Classic vignette:**  
A 68-year-old man with a 50-pack-year smoking history and known COPD (on tiotropium and salbutamol) presents with 2 days of worsening shortness of breath, productive cough with *increased purulence and volume*, and audible wheeze. He is sitting upright in the waiting area, using his accessory muscles. His respiratory rate is 28, oxygen saturations 86% on room air, and he can only speak in short sentences. He had a similar episode last winter requiring antibiotics and oral steroids.

**Dissect the clues:**
- *Age >60 + heavy smoking history* → high pre-test probability for COPD
- *Known COPD on long-acting bronchodilator* → chronic disease, now decompensated
- *Worsening dyspnea over days* → acute-on-chronic process
- *Increased purulence and volume of sputum* → triggers the clinical diagnosis of an exacerbation (Anthonisen type I)
- *Sitting upright, accessory muscle use, tripod position* → severe air trapping and increased work of breathing
- *RR 28, O2 sat 86%* → hypoxemic respiratory failure (type 1 or mixed)
- *Short sentences* → moderate-to-severe dyspnea (Borg scale ≥5)
- *Previous similar episodes responding to steroids/antibiotics* → pattern of recurrent exacerbations

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
1. **Silent chest** – No wheeze heard because of very severe airflow obstruction; the patient appears exhausted with a declining respiratory rate. This is a pre-arrest sign.
2. **Confusion or drowsiness** – A sign of hypercapnic encephalopathy (CO₂ narcosis), easily mistaken for delirium or stroke in elderly patients.
3. **Isolated tachypnea with no cough** – May be a “dry” exacerbation from environmental trigger (e.g., cold air, smoke) rather than infection; still requires similar management.
sections.2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Ma:
**Plain-language definition:**  
A COPD exacerbation is an acute worsening of respiratory symptoms that goes beyond the patient’s usual day-to-day variation and requires a change in management. It is typically triggered by respiratory infections (viral or bacterial) or environmental irritants, leading to increased airway inflammation, mucus production, and bronchoconstriction.

**Why it matters clinically:**
- **Frequency:** COPD exacerbations account for over 1 million hospital admissions per year in the US UK; they are the second most common cause of emergency medical admission after chest pain.
- **Stakes:** Each moderate-to-severe exacerbation accelerates decline in lung function, increases risk of cardiovascular events, and carries a 30-day mortality of ~10% (and up to 30% if requiring ICU).
- **Common pitfalls:** Mistaking exacerbation for acute left ventricular failure (LVF) can lead to inappropriate diuresis and worsening obstruction; under‑treating exacerbation with insufficient steroids/antibiotics leads to prolonged hospital stay and relapse.
sections.3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind Th:
**The story of how it develops:**  
In COPD, chronic inflammation has already destroyed alveolar walls (emphysema) and thickened airway walls (chronic bronchitis). An acute trigger—usually viral or bacterial infection—adds **more inflammation** (neutrophils, cytokines) on top of the chronic baseline. This causes:
- **Mucosal oedema** and **increased mucus secretion** → airway narrowing
- **Bronchospasm** (smooth muscle contraction) → dynamic hyperinflation
- **Mucus plugging** → atelectasis and V/Q mismatch

**Why each key clinical sign happens (mechanism → sign):**

| Sign | Mechanism |
|------|-----------|
| Wheeze | Turbulent airflow through narrowed airways during expiration |
| Prolonged expiration | Air trapping due to loss of elastic recoil + increased resistance |
| Accessory muscle use | Diaphragm is flattened by hyperinflation; intercostals and neck muscles recruited to generate negative pressure |
| Pursed-lip breathing | Creates positive end-expiratory pressure (auto-PEEP) to keep small airways open |
| Hypoxemia | V/Q mismatch—poorly ventilated alveoli are still perfused |
| Hypercapnia | Severe obstruction + tachypnea → inability to clear CO₂; may be exacerbated by high FiO₂ (O₂-induced hypercapnia via Haldane effect and reduced hypoxic drive) |
| Raised JVP | Increased intrathoracic pressure impedes venous return; also may indicate cor pulmonale (right heart strain) |

**Connect to pattern recognition cues:**  
The tripod position and accessory muscle use reflect the mechanical disadvantage of hyperinflation—the patient is literally fighting to exhale. Confusion or drowsiness tells you that CO₂ has risen high enough to depress consciousness (CO₂ >8–10 kPa). A silent chest means airflow is so sluggish that no wheeze is generated; this is a “code blue” until proven otherwise.
sections.4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins):
| Category | Common Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|--------------|-----------|
| **Infectious (most common – ~70-80%)** | Viral (rhinovirus, RSV, influenza) | Preceding coryza, sore throat, fever; winter months |
| | Bacterial (H. influenzae, S. pneumoniae, M. catarrhalis) | Purulent sputum change (green/yellow) with volume increase |
| **Environmental** | Air pollution, cold air, smoke | Onset after weather change or exposure to smoke/fumes |
| **Non-adherence** | Missing regular inhalers, stopping LAMA/LABA | History of frequent relapses, inhaler technique poor |
| **Iatrogenic** | Sedatives (benzodiazepines, opioids) that depress respiratory drive | Patient has recently used sleeping pills or painkillers |
| **Other medical** | Pneumonia, pneumothorax, pleural effusion, pulmonary embolism | Unilateral signs, pleuritic pain, sudden onset, haemoptysis |
| **Idiopathic** | No clear trigger; often denotation of disease progression | Recurrent exacerbations without apparent cause |

**Most common cause:** Upper respiratory viral infection (rhinovirus) followed by secondary bacterial infection.
sections.5. Investigations — What, Why, What It M:
**Diagnostic criteria** (GOLD definition): Acute worsening of respiratory symptoms that is beyond normal day-to-day variation and leads to a change in therapy. No single test confirms; it is a clinical diagnosis.

**Arterial blood gas (ABG)** – *Why:* Assess oxygenation, ventilation, and acid-base status. *What it shows:*
- Type 1 respiratory failure: PaO₂ <8 kPa with normal/low PaCO₂ (early/moderate)
- Type 2 respiratory failure: PaO₂ <8 kPa AND PaCO₂ >6.5 kPa (severe)
- Respiratory acidosis: pH <7.35 with PaCO₂ >6.5 (indicates acute-on-chronic hypercapnia)
- Bicarbonate (HCO₃⁻) helps distinguish acute from chronic; if HCO₃ >30, suggests chronic compensation (long-standing hypercapnia)

**Chest X-ray** – *Why:* Exclude pneumothorax, pneumonia, pulmonary oedema, effusion. *Pattern recognition:* Hyperinflation (flat hemidiaphragms >7th rib anteriorly, increased retrosternal airspace) suggests COPD; new consolidation → pneumonia trigger; pneumothorax needs urgent drainage.

**Sputum culture / blood cultures** – *Why:* Guide antibiotic therapy if purulent sputum. *Caution:* Sputum may be unobtainable; start empirical antibiotics based on local guidelines.

**Full blood count** – *Why:* Look for leukocytosis (infection) or anaemia (may mimic or worsen breathlessness). *Polycythaemia (Hb >16g/dL)* suggests chronic hypoxaemia (secondary to COPD).

**ECG** – *Why:* Rule out acute coronary syndrome / LVF. *Pattern:* Right axis deviation, P pulmonale (peaked P-wave in II/III/AVF) → cor pulmonale; sinus tachycardia common.

**NT-proBNP** – Consider if clinical doubt for heart failure; >400 pg/mL suggests LVF, but can overlap (COPD exacerbation itself may raise BNP due to right heart strain).

**Single finding that confirms:** Not one, but **purulent sputum + ABG showing acute type 2 respiratory failure** is highly suggestive of a severe exacerbation requiring non-invasive ventilation (NIV).
sections.6. Management — Approach & Reasoning:
**Immediate (first hour) – stabilise and assess severity:**

1. **Oxygen** – Target SpO₂ 88–92% (in COPD, avoid high FiO₂ → risk of O₂-induced hypercapnia). Start with 24% Venturi mask or 1–2 L/min nasal cannula. *Rationale:* Titrate to saturations, not to normoxia; hypoxic drive is a myth in most COPD patients, but high FiO₂ can worsen V/Q mismatch by blunting hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction.

2. **Bronchodilators** – **Salbutamol** 5 mg via nebuliser (oxygen-driven) + **Ipratropium** 0.5 mg via nebuliser (air-driven if repeat doses). *Why:* Rapidly reverse acute bronchoconstriction; combination is more effective than either alone. Repeat every 20–30 min as needed in severe cases.

3. **Corticosteroids** – **Prednisolone** 30–40 mg oral once daily for 5 days (or IV hydrocortisone 200 mg stat if unable to swallow). *Why:* Reduce airway inflammation – shortens recovery time, reduces risk of relapse. Avoid long courses (>5–7 days) due to hyperglycaemia, myopathy.

4. **Consider non-invasive ventilation (NIV)** – If pH <7.35 and PaCO₂ >6.5 despite initial therapy. *Set up:* BiPAP, IPAP 10–20 cmH₂O, EPAP 4–6 cmH₂O. *Why:* Unloads respiratory muscles, improves alveolar ventilation, buys time for steroids to work.

**Ward (24–72 hours) – continue and escalate if needed:**

- **Antibiotics:** If purulent sputum OR clinical signs of infection (fever, leukocytosis) → **Amoxicillin** 500 mg TDS oral (or **Doxycycline** 200 mg stat then 100 mg OD if penicillin allergy) for 5 days. *Rationale:* Bacterial exacerbation common; early antibiotics reduce treatment failure.
- **Ongoing bronchodilators:** Nebulisers 4–6 hourly then step down to inhalers as patient improves. Check inhaler technique.
- **Monitor for NIV failure:** If pH not improving after 1–2 hours, or patient unable to tolerate, escalate to ICU for invasive ventilation.
- **Venous thromboembolism prophylaxis:** All COPD exacerbation inpatients should receive LMWH (e.g., dalteparin 5000 units SC OD) unless contraindicated.

**Long-term (discharge planning):**

- **Smoking cessation** – NRT, varenicline, or bupropion; referral to stop-smoking service.
- **Optimise inhaler regimen** – LAMA/LABA combination (e.g., tiotropium + indacaterol) ± ICS (if previous exacerbations). Check technique.
- **Pulmonary rehabilitation** – Refer for 8-week programme; improves exercise capacity, reduces future admissions.
- **Vaccinations** – Annual influenza and pneumococcal (PCV13 + PPSV23).
- **Home oxygen if appropriate** – PaO₂ <7.3 kPa while stable on two occasions >3 weeks apart → long-term oxygen therapy (LTOT).

**What NOT to do:**
- **Do not give high-flow O₂ (e.g., non-rebreather mask at 15 L/min)** – risks CO₂ retention, depressed consciousness, respiratory arrest.
- **Do not rely on “nebulisers via air” without oxygen in acute setting** – patients may desaturate during treatment; use oxygen-driven nebs initially.
- **Do not routinely use mucolytics** (e.g., carbocisteine) in acute exacerbation – only indicated in stable COPD with chronic productive cough.
- **Do not discharge without ensuring inhaler competence** – common cause of early readmission.
sections.7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison T:
| Feature | COPD Exacerbation | Acute Heart Failure (LVF) | Acute Asthma | Pneumonia |
|---------|-------------------|---------------------------|--------------|-----------|
| **Pain** | Usually none; may have chest tightness | Chest “pressure” or discomfort; can be associated with MI | None; sometimes chest tightness | Pleuritic pain if pleural involvement |
| **Exam** | Pursed-lip breathing, wheeze, prolonged expiration, hyperinflation | Crackles (bilateral basal), S3 gallop, raised JVP, pitting oedema | Polyphonic expiratory wheeze, hyperinflated, accessory muscle use | Focal crackles, bronchial breathing, reduced expansion over the affected lobe |
| **ECG** | Sinus tachycardia, signs of cor pulmonale (right axis, P pulmonale) | Usually sinus tachy; may show LV strain, AF, or old MI | Sinus tachycardia only (during attack) | Tachycardia; possibly non-specific ST-T changes |
| **Labs** | ABG: type 1 or 2 RF; HCO₃ normal/high if chronic | NT-proBNP >400; ABG: type 1 RF, low PaCO₂ (due to hyperventilation) | ABG: usually normal; can have low PaCO₂ (respiratory alkalosis); severe attack → type 1 RF | CRP/ESR elevated; WBC elevated; sputum/blood cultures positive; ABG: often type 1 RF |
| **Key diff** | History of smoking, chronic cough/sputum, barrel chest; symptoms develop over days | Orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, history of IHD/HTN; crackles that clear with diuretics | Younger age, atopy, intermittent symptoms, often normal between attacks; responds rapidly to bronchodilators | Fever, focal signs on exam + CXR infiltrate; usually more acute onset (1–2 days) |
sections.8. Complications — What If You Miss It?:
**1. Type 2 Respiratory Failure with CO₂ Narcosis**
- *Presentation:* Drowsiness, confusion, asterixis (flapping tremor), bounding pulse, warm peripheries (peripheral vasodilation from hypercapnia).
- *Key sign:* PaCO₂ >8 kPa with pH <7.25.
- *What to do:* Start NIV immediately; if pH <7.15 or GCS falling → call ICU for invasive ventilation.

**2. Pneumothorax**
- *Presentation:* Sudden onset pleuritic chest pain + increased dyspnoea in a known COPD patient; may be spontaneous (bleb rupture). Exam: hyper-resonance, reduced breath sounds, tracheal deviation if tension.
- *Key sign:* On CXR, visceral pleural line. Tension: hypotension, distended neck veins.
- *What to do:* Needle decompression (2nd intercostal space midclavicular line) then chest drain. In COPD, even a small pneumothorax can cause severe hypoxia.

**3. Cor Pulmonale (Right Heart Failure)**
- *Presentation:* Worsening peripheral oedema, raised JVP, hepatomegaly, tricuspid regurgitation murmur.
- *Key sign:* ECG shows P pulmonale, right ventricular hypertrophy; echo shows right ventricular systolic dysfunction.
- *What to do:* Treat exacerbation aggressively; consider long-term oxygen (LTOT) if persistently hypoxic; diuretics for fluid overload, but avoid overdiuresis (can cause metabolic alkalosis → depressed respiratory drive).

**4. ICU Admission / Invasive Ventilation**
- *Indications:* Failed NIV (pH falling, inability to clear secretions, haemodynamic instability), cardiac arrest, severe encephalopathy.
- *Late presentation pattern:* The exhausted patient with a silent chest who suddenly becomes bradycardic and hypotensive – this is the “missed opportunity” where earlier escalation could have prevented cardiorespiratory arrest.
sections.9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls:
**Mnemonics:**

- **“COPD” management in the acute setting**  
  C = Controlled O₂ (88–92%)  
  O = Oral/IV bronchodilators (salbutamol + ipratropium)  
  P = Prednisolone  
  D = Do not forget to consider NIV if pH low

- **“HAPPY” for causes of COPD exacerbation**  
  H = H. influenzae (most common bacteria)  
  A = Air pollution  
  P = Pneumococcus / Pseudomonas (in severe cases)  
  P = Parainfluenza / RSV (viral triggers)  
  Y = You forgot to take your inhalers (non-adherence)

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**
1. **The tripod position** – a patient leaning forward with arms on knees is a clear sign of moderate-to-severe COPD exacerbation; do not confuse with orthopnea of LVF (which prefers sitting upright but not leaning forward).
2. **Pursed-lip breathing** – is a compensatory mechanism; its absence in a tachypnoeic patient may indicate fatigue (impending need for NIV).
3. **Baseline COPD severity matters** – a patient with FEV₁ <30% predicted classically develops hypercapnia more easily; a single ABG with PaCO₂ 7 kPa in such patients is an emergency, not a “chronic” finding.
4. **O₂ saturation targets are 88–92%** – fixed, not just “aim for >90%”. Saturating >92% without knowing why (e.g., on high-flow O₂) is a red flag for possible CO₂ retention.
5. **The “best” test for bacterial vs viral exacerbation is sputum examination** – but in practice, start antibiotics if sputum is purulent (Anothonisen criteria). Do not wait for cultures.
6. **NIV in COPD reduces need for intubation and mortality** – but must be started early (within 2 hours of failing medical therapy). A 1-hour trial of NIV that does not improve pH → escalate.
7. **Look for pink puffy appearance on high-dose steroids** – always check blood glucose; corticosteroids can cause hyperglycaemia even in non-diabetics.

**Common exam / ward mistakes to avoid:**
- Giving high-concentration O₂ “just to get the saturations up” – this kills COPD patients.
- Prescribing benzodiazepines for anxiety related to breathlessness – depresses respiratory drive.
- Starting empirical antibiotics for every exacerbation – if sputum is clear/mucoid and no fever, treat with steroids alone.
- Discharging on the same inhaled regimen that failed before – review and escalate to LAMA/LABA/ICS.
sections.10. Escalation Criteria:
**When to call the senior (registrar / attending):**
- pH <7.35 and PaCO₂ >6.5 despite 1 hour of optimal medical therapy (steroids, bronchodilators, O₂)
- Sustained NEWS2 score ≥5 (respiratory rate ≥25, O₂ sat <90% on O₂, systolic BP <90 or >200, heart rate >130 or <40, consciousness new confusion)
- New onset of confusion or drowsiness
- Unable to tolerate NIV or no improvement after 2 hours

**When to call ICU / crash team:**
- pH <7.15 (critical acidosis)
- PaCO₂ >10 kPa
- GCS ≤8
- Hypotension (systolic <80 mmHg) not due to volume depletion
- Respiratory rate rising (or falling) with signs of exhaustion
- Cardiac arrhythmia (e.g., new atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response)
- Failed NIV (pH deteriorating, intubation considered)

**Specific thresholds (NEWS2):**
- Oxygen saturation in COPD (target range 88–92%): a score of 3 is assigned if SpO₂ <92% on O₂, but **do not suppress the alarm by giving more O₂** – assess the patient, re-check ABG, call for help.
- **The “Red Flag” escalation:** any patient who required NIV within the last 12 hours should remain on continuous monitoring and have a low threshold for ICU review.

**Bottom line for the junior doctor:**  
COPD exacerbation is a clinical diagnosis that can kill quickly. The two pillars of safe management are **controlled oxygen** and **early use of NIV** for those with respiratory acidosis. Pattern recognition—the tripod, pursed lips, purulent sputum—will serve you far better than a single test. When in doubt, ABG.

## COPD — Exacerbation
**One-liner:** Acute worsening of chronic airflow limitation, usually triggered by infection, causing dyspnea, cough, and sputum change; a leading cause of emergency admissions.

### 1. Pattern Recognition — The Patient You'll See

**Classic vignette:**  
A 68-year-old man with a 50-pack-year smoking history and known COPD (on tiotropium and salbutamol) presents with 2 days of worsening shortness of breath, productive cough with *increased purulence and volume*, and audible wheeze. He is sitting upright in the waiting area, using his accessory muscles. His respiratory rate is 28, oxygen saturations 86% on room air, and he can only speak in short sentences. He had a similar episode last winter requiring antibiotics and oral steroids.

**Dissect the clues:**
- *Age >60 + heavy smoking history* → high pre-test probability for COPD
- *Known COPD on long-acting bronchodilator* → chronic disease, now decompensated
- *Worsening dyspnea over days* → acute-on-chronic process
- *Increased purulence and volume of sputum* → triggers the clinical diagnosis of an exacerbation (Anthonisen type I)
- *Sitting upright, accessory muscle use, tripod position* → severe air trapping and increased work of breathing
- *RR 28, O2 sat 86%* → hypoxemic respiratory failure (type 1 or mixed)
- *Short sentences* → moderate-to-severe dyspnea (Borg scale ≥5)
- *Previous similar episodes responding to steroids/antibiotics* → pattern of recurrent exacerbations

**Atypical presentations that may trick you:**
1. **Silent chest** – No wheeze heard because of very severe airflow obstruction; the patient appears exhausted with a declining respiratory rate. This is a pre-arrest sign.
2. **Confusion or drowsiness** – A sign of hypercapnic encephalopathy (CO₂ narcosis), easily mistaken for delirium or stroke in elderly patients.
3. **Isolated tachypnea with no cough** – May be a “dry” exacerbation from environmental trigger (e.g., cold air, smoke) rather than infection; still requires similar management.

### 2. What Is This? (Definition & Why It Matters)

**Plain-language definition:**  
A COPD exacerbation is an acute worsening of respiratory symptoms that goes beyond the patient’s usual day-to-day variation and requires a change in management. It is typically triggered by respiratory infections (viral or bacterial) or environmental irritants, leading to increased airway inflammation, mucus production, and bronchoconstriction.

**Why it matters clinically:**
- **Frequency:** COPD exacerbations account for over 1 million hospital admissions per year in the US UK; they are the second most common cause of emergency medical admission after chest pain.
- **Stakes:** Each moderate-to-severe exacerbation accelerates decline in lung function, increases risk of cardiovascular events, and carries a 30-day mortality of ~10% (and up to 30% if requiring ICU).
- **Common pitfalls:** Mistaking exacerbation for acute left ventricular failure (LVF) can lead to inappropriate diuresis and worsening obstruction; under‑treating exacerbation with insufficient steroids/antibiotics leads to prolonged hospital stay and relapse.

### 3. Pathophysiology — The “Why” Behind The Findings

**The story of how it develops:**  
In COPD, chronic inflammation has already destroyed alveolar walls (emphysema) and thickened airway walls (chronic bronchitis). An acute trigger—usually viral or bacterial infection—adds **more inflammation** (neutrophils, cytokines) on top of the chronic baseline. This causes:
- **Mucosal oedema** and **increased mucus secretion** → airway narrowing
- **Bronchospasm** (smooth muscle contraction) → dynamic hyperinflation
- **Mucus plugging** → atelectasis and V/Q mismatch

**Why each key clinical sign happens (mechanism → sign):**

| Sign | Mechanism |
|------|-----------|
| Wheeze | Turbulent airflow through narrowed airways during expiration |
| Prolonged expiration | Air trapping due to loss of elastic recoil + increased resistance |
| Accessory muscle use | Diaphragm is flattened by hyperinflation; intercostals and neck muscles recruited to generate negative pressure |
| Pursed-lip breathing | Creates positive end-expiratory pressure (auto-PEEP) to keep small airways open |
| Hypoxemia | V/Q mismatch—poorly ventilated alveoli are still perfused |
| Hypercapnia | Severe obstruction + tachypnea → inability to clear CO₂; may be exacerbated by high FiO₂ (O₂-induced hypercapnia via Haldane effect and reduced hypoxic drive) |
| Raised JVP | Increased intrathoracic pressure impedes venous return; also may indicate cor pulmonale (right heart strain) |

**Connect to pattern recognition cues:**  
The tripod position and accessory muscle use reflect the mechanical disadvantage of hyperinflation—the patient is literally fighting to exhale. Confusion or drowsiness tells you that CO₂ has risen high enough to depress consciousness (CO₂ >8–10 kPa). A silent chest means airflow is so sluggish that no wheeze is generated; this is a “code blue” until proven otherwise.

### 4. Causes / Triggers (organised in bins)

| Category | Common Cause | Stem Clue |
|----------|--------------|-----------|
| **Infectious (most common – ~70-80%)** | Viral (rhinovirus, RSV, influenza) | Preceding coryza, sore throat, fever; winter months |
| | Bacterial (H. influenzae, S. pneumoniae, M. catarrhalis) | Purulent sputum change (green/yellow) with volume increase |
| **Environmental** | Air pollution, cold air, smoke | Onset after weather change or exposure to smoke/fumes |
| **Non-adherence** | Missing regular inhalers, stopping LAMA/LABA | History of frequent relapses, inhaler technique poor |
| **Iatrogenic** | Sedatives (benzodiazepines, opioids) that depress respiratory drive | Patient has recently used sleeping pills or painkillers |
| **Other medical** | Pneumonia, pneumothorax, pleural effusion, pulmonary embolism | Unilateral signs, pleuritic pain, sudden onset, haemoptysis |
| **Idiopathic** | No clear trigger; often denotation of disease progression | Recurrent exacerbations without apparent cause |

**Most common cause:** Upper respiratory viral infection (rhinovirus) followed by secondary bacterial infection.

### 5. Investigations — What, Why, What It Means

**Diagnostic criteria** (GOLD definition): Acute worsening of respiratory symptoms that is beyond normal day-to-day variation and leads to a change in therapy. No single test confirms; it is a clinical diagnosis.

**Arterial blood gas (ABG)** – *Why:* Assess oxygenation, ventilation, and acid-base status. *What it shows:*
- Type 1 respiratory failure: PaO₂ <8 kPa with normal/low PaCO₂ (early/moderate)
- Type 2 respiratory failure: PaO₂ <8 kPa AND PaCO₂ >6.5 kPa (severe)
- Respiratory acidosis: pH <7.35 with PaCO₂ >6.5 (indicates acute-on-chronic hypercapnia)
- Bicarbonate (HCO₃⁻) helps distinguish acute from chronic; if HCO₃ >30, suggests chronic compensation (long-standing hypercapnia)

**Chest X-ray** – *Why:* Exclude pneumothorax, pneumonia, pulmonary oedema, effusion. *Pattern recognition:* Hyperinflation (flat hemidiaphragms >7th rib anteriorly, increased retrosternal airspace) suggests COPD; new consolidation → pneumonia trigger; pneumothorax needs urgent drainage.

**Sputum culture / blood cultures** – *Why:* Guide antibiotic therapy if purulent sputum. *Caution:* Sputum may be unobtainable; start empirical antibiotics based on local guidelines.

**Full blood count** – *Why:* Look for leukocytosis (infection) or anaemia (may mimic or worsen breathlessness). *Polycythaemia (Hb >16g/dL)* suggests chronic hypoxaemia (secondary to COPD).

**ECG** – *Why:* Rule out acute coronary syndrome / LVF. *Pattern:* Right axis deviation, P pulmonale (peaked P-wave in II/III/AVF) → cor pulmonale; sinus tachycardia common.

**NT-proBNP** – Consider if clinical doubt for heart failure; >400 pg/mL suggests LVF, but can overlap (COPD exacerbation itself may raise BNP due to right heart strain).

**Single finding that confirms:** Not one, but **purulent sputum + ABG showing acute type 2 respiratory failure** is highly suggestive of a severe exacerbation requiring non-invasive ventilation (NIV).

### 6. Management — Approach & Reasoning

**Immediate (first hour) – stabilise and assess severity:**

1. **Oxygen** – Target SpO₂ 88–92% (in COPD, avoid high FiO₂ → risk of O₂-induced hypercapnia). Start with 24% Venturi mask or 1–2 L/min nasal cannula. *Rationale:* Titrate to saturations, not to normoxia; hypoxic drive is a myth in most COPD patients, but high FiO₂ can worsen V/Q mismatch by blunting hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction.

2. **Bronchodilators** – **Salbutamol** 5 mg via nebuliser (oxygen-driven) + **Ipratropium** 0.5 mg via nebuliser (air-driven if repeat doses). *Why:* Rapidly reverse acute bronchoconstriction; combination is more effective than either alone. Repeat every 20–30 min as needed in severe cases.

3. **Corticosteroids** – **Prednisolone** 30–40 mg oral once daily for 5 days (or IV hydrocortisone 200 mg stat if unable to swallow). *Why:* Reduce airway inflammation – shortens recovery time, reduces risk of relapse. Avoid long courses (>5–7 days) due to hyperglycaemia, myopathy.

4. **Consider non-invasive ventilation (NIV)** – If pH <7.35 and PaCO₂ >6.5 despite initial therapy. *Set up:* BiPAP, IPAP 10–20 cmH₂O, EPAP 4–6 cmH₂O. *Why:* Unloads respiratory muscles, improves alveolar ventilation, buys time for steroids to work.

**Ward (24–72 hours) – continue and escalate if needed:**

- **Antibiotics:** If purulent sputum OR clinical signs of infection (fever, leukocytosis) → **Amoxicillin** 500 mg TDS oral (or **Doxycycline** 200 mg stat then 100 mg OD if penicillin allergy) for 5 days. *Rationale:* Bacterial exacerbation common; early antibiotics reduce treatment failure.
- **Ongoing bronchodilators:** Nebulisers 4–6 hourly then step down to inhalers as patient improves. Check inhaler technique.
- **Monitor for NIV failure:** If pH not improving after 1–2 hours, or patient unable to tolerate, escalate to ICU for invasive ventilation.
- **Venous thromboembolism prophylaxis:** All COPD exacerbation inpatients should receive LMWH (e.g., dalteparin 5000 units SC OD) unless contraindicated.

**Long-term (discharge planning):**

- **Smoking cessation** – NRT, varenicline, or bupropion; referral to stop-smoking service.
- **Optimise inhaler regimen** – LAMA/LABA combination (e.g., tiotropium + indacaterol) ± ICS (if previous exacerbations). Check technique.
- **Pulmonary rehabilitation** – Refer for 8-week programme; improves exercise capacity, reduces future admissions.
- **Vaccinations** – Annual influenza and pneumococcal (PCV13 + PPSV23).
- **Home oxygen if appropriate** – PaO₂ <7.3 kPa while stable on two occasions >3 weeks apart → long-term oxygen therapy (LTOT).

**What NOT to do:**
- **Do not give high-flow O₂ (e.g., non-rebreather mask at 15 L/min)** – risks CO₂ retention, depressed consciousness, respiratory arrest.
- **Do not rely on “nebulisers via air” without oxygen in acute setting** – patients may desaturate during treatment; use oxygen-driven nebs initially.
- **Do not routinely use mucolytics** (e.g., carbocisteine) in acute exacerbation – only indicated in stable COPD with chronic productive cough.
- **Do not discharge without ensuring inhaler competence** – common cause of early readmission.

### 7. Differential Diagnosis — Comparison Table

| Feature | COPD Exacerbation | Acute Heart Failure (LVF) | Acute Asthma | Pneumonia |
|---------|-------------------|---------------------------|--------------|-----------|
| **Pain** | Usually none; may have chest tightness | Chest “pressure” or discomfort; can be associated with MI | None; sometimes chest tightness | Pleuritic pain if pleural involvement |
| **Exam** | Pursed-lip breathing, wheeze, prolonged expiration, hyperinflation | Crackles (bilateral basal), S3 gallop, raised JVP, pitting oedema | Polyphonic expiratory wheeze, hyperinflated, accessory muscle use | Focal crackles, bronchial breathing, reduced expansion over the affected lobe |
| **ECG** | Sinus tachycardia, signs of cor pulmonale (right axis, P pulmonale) | Usually sinus tachy; may show LV strain, AF, or old MI | Sinus tachycardia only (during attack) | Tachycardia; possibly non-specific ST-T changes |
| **Labs** | ABG: type 1 or 2 RF; HCO₃ normal/high if chronic | NT-proBNP >400; ABG: type 1 RF, low PaCO₂ (due to hyperventilation) | ABG: usually normal; can have low PaCO₂ (respiratory alkalosis); severe attack → type 1 RF | CRP/ESR elevated; WBC elevated; sputum/blood cultures positive; ABG: often type 1 RF |
| **Key diff** | History of smoking, chronic cough/sputum, barrel chest; symptoms develop over days | Orthopnea, paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea, history of IHD/HTN; crackles that clear with diuretics | Younger age, atopy, intermittent symptoms, often normal between attacks; responds rapidly to bronchodilators | Fever, focal signs on exam + CXR infiltrate; usually more acute onset (1–2 days) |

### 8. Complications — What If You Miss It?

**1. Type 2 Respiratory Failure with CO₂ Narcosis**
- *Presentation:* Drowsiness, confusion, asterixis (flapping tremor), bounding pulse, warm peripheries (peripheral vasodilation from hypercapnia).
- *Key sign:* PaCO₂ >8 kPa with pH <7.25.
- *What to do:* Start NIV immediately; if pH <7.15 or GCS falling → call ICU for invasive ventilation.

**2. Pneumothorax**
- *Presentation:* Sudden onset pleuritic chest pain + increased dyspnoea in a known COPD patient; may be spontaneous (bleb rupture). Exam: hyper-resonance, reduced breath sounds, tracheal deviation if tension.
- *Key sign:* On CXR, visceral pleural line. Tension: hypotension, distended neck veins.
- *What to do:* Needle decompression (2nd intercostal space midclavicular line) then chest drain. In COPD, even a small pneumothorax can cause severe hypoxia.

**3. Cor Pulmonale (Right Heart Failure)**
- *Presentation:* Worsening peripheral oedema, raised JVP, hepatomegaly, tricuspid regurgitation murmur.
- *Key sign:* ECG shows P pulmonale, right ventricular hypertrophy; echo shows right ventricular systolic dysfunction.
- *What to do:* Treat exacerbation aggressively; consider long-term oxygen (LTOT) if persistently hypoxic; diuretics for fluid overload, but avoid overdiuresis (can cause metabolic alkalosis → depressed respiratory drive).

**4. ICU Admission / Invasive Ventilation**
- *Indications:* Failed NIV (pH falling, inability to clear secretions, haemodynamic instability), cardiac arrest, severe encephalopathy.
- *Late presentation pattern:* The exhausted patient with a silent chest who suddenly becomes bradycardic and hypotensive – this is the “missed opportunity” where earlier escalation could have prevented cardiorespiratory arrest.

### 9. High-Yield Mnemonics & Pearls

**Mnemonics:**

- **“COPD” management in the acute setting**  
  C = Controlled O₂ (88–92%)  
  O = Oral/IV bronchodilators (salbutamol + ipratropium)  
  P = Prednisolone  
  D = Do not forget to consider NIV if pH low

- **“HAPPY” for causes of COPD exacerbation**  
  H = H. influenzae (most common bacteria)  
  A = Air pollution  
  P = Pneumococcus / Pseudomonas (in severe cases)  
  P = Parainfluenza / RSV (viral triggers)  
  Y = You forgot to take your inhalers (non-adherence)

**Clinical pearls (pattern-recognition cues):**
1. **The tripod position** – a patient leaning forward with arms on knees is a clear sign of moderate-to-severe COPD exacerbation; do not confuse with orthopnea of LVF (which prefers sitting upright but not leaning forward).
2. **Pursed-lip breathing** – is a compensatory mechanism; its absence in a tachypnoeic patient may indicate fatigue (impending need for NIV).
3. **Baseline COPD severity matters** – a patient with FEV₁ <30% predicted classically develops hypercapnia more easily; a single ABG with PaCO₂ 7 kPa in such patients is an emergency, not a “chronic” finding.
4. **O₂ saturation targets are 88–92%** – fixed, not just “aim for >90%”. Saturating >92% without knowing why (e.g., on high-flow O₂) is a red flag for possible CO₂ retention.
5. **The “best” test for bacterial vs viral exacerbation is sputum examination** – but in practice, start antibiotics if sputum is purulent (Anothonisen criteria). Do not wait for cultures.
6. **NIV in COPD reduces need for intubation and mortality** – but must be started early (within 2 hours of failing medical therapy). A 1-hour trial of NIV that does not improve pH → escalate.
7. **Look for pink puffy appearance on high-dose steroids** – always check blood glucose; corticosteroids can cause hyperglycaemia even in non-diabetics.

**Common exam / ward mistakes to avoid:**
- Giving high-concentration O₂ “just to get the saturations up” – this kills COPD patients.
- Prescribing benzodiazepines for anxiety related to breathlessness – depresses respiratory drive.
- Starting empirical antibiotics for every exacerbation – if sputum is clear/mucoid and no fever, treat with steroids alone.
- Discharging on the same inhaled regimen that failed before – review and escalate to LAMA/LABA/ICS.

### 10. Escalation Criteria

**When to call the senior (registrar / attending):**
- pH <7.35 and PaCO₂ >6.5 despite 1 hour of optimal medical therapy (steroids, bronchodilators, O₂)
- Sustained NEWS2 score ≥5 (respiratory rate ≥25, O₂ sat <90% on O₂, systolic BP <90 or >200, heart rate >130 or <40, consciousness new confusion)
- New onset of confusion or drowsiness
- Unable to tolerate NIV or no improvement after 2 hours

**When to call ICU / crash team:**
- pH <7.15 (critical acidosis)
- PaCO₂ >10 kPa
- GCS ≤8
- Hypotension (systolic <80 mmHg) not due to volume depletion
- Respiratory rate rising (or falling) with signs of exhaustion
- Cardiac arrhythmia (e.g., new atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response)
- Failed NIV (pH deteriorating, intubation considered)

**Specific thresholds (NEWS2):**
- Oxygen saturation in COPD (target range 88–92%): a score of 3 is assigned if SpO₂ <92% on O₂, but **do not suppress the alarm by giving more O₂** – assess the patient, re-check ABG, call for help.
- **The “Red Flag” escalation:** any patient who required NIV within the last 12 hours should remain on continuous monitoring and have a low threshold for ICU review.

**Bottom line for the junior doctor:**  
COPD exacerbation is a clinical diagnosis that can kill quickly. The two pillars of safe management are **controlled oxygen** and **early use of NIV** for those with respiratory acidosis. Pattern recognition—the tripod, pursed lips, purulent sputum—will serve you far better than a single test. When in doubt, ABG.
